title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Cyberbullying and Online Harassment
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps survivors of cyberbullying reclaim their nervous system and heal from online trauma.
body:
Understanding the Trauma of Cyberbullying and Online Harassment

Cyberbullying and online harassment are not just unpleasant experiences; they are forms of trauma that deeply affect the nervous system. Unlike traditional bullying, the internet’s permanent, pervasive, and anonymous nature can intensify the impact, often leaving survivors feeling unsafe even in their own homes. The invisible wounds of cyberbullying manifest somatically—through chronic stress, hypervigilance, panic attacks, or dissociation.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, a leading trauma researcher, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body and nervous system, not just in the mind. This means that cognitive therapies alone may not fully address the aftereffects of online harassment. Survivors often feel stuck, replaying the trauma in their bodies, unable to regulate their stress responses effectively.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Isn’t Enough

Many survivors of cyberbullying seek traditional talk therapy hoping to ‘talk it out,’ but find themselves feeling stuck or retraumatized. That’s because trauma rewires the brain’s threat detection systems and dysregulates the autonomic nervous system (ANS). The ANS governs our fight, flight, freeze, and shutdown reactions.

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, explains that trauma involves a disruption in the body's natural ability to discharge excess energy from a threat. Simply talking about the experience does not always enable this release, which leaves somatic symptoms and emotional distress unresolved.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a body-based approach that focuses on tuning into physical sensations and nervous system states to process and resolve trauma. Instead of only discussing thoughts and emotions, somatic therapy engages the body’s natural capacity to regulate and heal itself.

Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory provides the scientific framework for understanding how somatic therapy helps. It highlights the role of the vagus nerve in shifting us out of defensive survival states into social engagement and calm. Somatic therapy helps survivors recognize nervous system cues and learn to self-regulate, restoring a sense of safety.

How Cyberbullying Affects the Nervous System

Cyberbullying triggers a chronic state of hyperarousal or shutdown in the nervous system. The persistent nature of online harassment means survivors often remain in fight, flight, or freeze modes long after the interaction ends. This chronic stress can lead to symptoms such as:

  • Increased heart rate and muscle tension
  • Insomnia and fatigue
  • Heightened anxiety and panic attacks
  • Dissociation or emotional numbness
  • Difficulty concentrating and memory problems

Dr. Deb Dana, a clinical social worker specializing in Polyvagal Theory, notes that these responses are the nervous system’s way of trying to protect the body from perceived threat, even when the threat is digital rather than physical.

Somatic Therapy Techniques for Healing Cyberbullying Trauma

Somatic therapy offers practical tools to help survivors reconnect with their bodies and regulate their nervous systems. Some effective methods include:

  • Body Awareness Exercises: Noticing sensations like tension, warmth, or tingling helps survivors understand how trauma manifests physically.
  • Grounding Techniques: Practices such as feeling feet on the floor or placing hands on the body anchor survivors in the present moment and promote safety.
  • Breath Regulation: Slow, diaphragmatic breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing stress.
  • Movement and Tremoring: Following Peter Levine’s work, small involuntary body movements help discharge trapped energy.
  • Safe Place Visualization: Imagining a secure environment can downregulate hypervigilance.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, integrates these techniques with mindful movement and touch to help clients renegotiate traumatic memories through their bodies.

Building a New Relationship with the Online World

Healing from cyberbullying also involves reclaiming one’s relationship with technology and social media. Somatic therapy encourages survivors to recognize their nervous system’s limits and establish boundaries that support regulation, such as:

  • Setting specific times for checking messages or social media
  • Using grounding practices before and after online activity
  • Creating digital detox periods to allow nervous system recovery
  • Curating safe online spaces and supportive communities

Gabor Maté highlights the importance of compassionate self-awareness during this process, encouraging survivors to treat themselves with kindness rather than judgment.

The Role of Somatic Therapy in Long-Term Recovery

Somatic therapy is not a quick fix but a powerful approach for long-term nervous system regulation and trauma resolution. Dan Siegel’s concept of mindsight—the ability to perceive one’s own internal experience—aligns well with somatic therapy’s focus on body awareness and integration.

Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) therapy, often combined with somatic approaches, can further help survivors reprocess traumatic memories stored in the body. Together, these therapies help survivors regain a sense of control, safety, and resilience.

“Healing trauma means engaging the body’s wisdom, not just the mind’s narrative.” – Bessel van der Kolk

Finding Support and Taking the Next Steps

If you are a survivor of cyberbullying or online harassment struggling with persistent stress or trauma symptoms, consider exploring somatic therapy with a qualified practitioner. Look for therapists trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Polyvagal-informed care. These approaches honor the body’s role in trauma healing.

Remember, your nervous system is designed to heal and regulate. With patience, practice, and support, you can reclaim your sense of safety and presence—both on and offline.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on body sensations and nervous system regulation, whereas traditional talk therapy primarily addresses thoughts and emotions. This helps release trauma stored physically.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help with anxiety caused by online harassment?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapy teaches nervous system regulation techniques that reduce hyperarousal and anxiety triggered by cyberbullying and online threats.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy effective for trauma caused by non-physical threats like cyberbullying?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Trauma impacts the nervous system regardless of physical injury, and somatic therapy addresses the body-based symptoms of digital trauma.

faqs.q: How do I find a somatic therapist trained in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Look for certifications in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or training in Polyvagal Theory applications. Many therapists list specialties online.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic approaches aid healing.

metaDescription: Learn how somatic therapy helps survivors of cyberbullying heal by regulating the nervous system and releasing trauma stored in the body.
author: The Oracle Lover
